Information about the OQS project is available at <https://openquantumsafe.org>. The project dashboard is at <https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html>.

## OQS Technical Steering Committee

Rodrigo noted ongoing pending items: the open security issues (including review of security-related content in both liboqs and oqs-provider), the removal of the security email list ([issue #293](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/293)), and the Q2 health report. Rodrigo also noted that a corresponding SECURITY.md already exists in liboqs and will verify whether it requires similar updates.

> [!IMPORTANT]

### Trail of Bits draft PRs ([#2477](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2477), [#2478](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2478), [#2479](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2479), [#2480](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2480))

These remain as draft PRs and are not considered a top priority given the team's current focus on the 0.17 release cycle and Round 3 algorithm inclusion. Bruce noted he has not had time to review them in depth yet. Michael observed that some of them — particularly the public-input fuzzing CI ([#2478](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2478)) and API mutation testing CI ([#2479](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2479)) — appear interesting and go beyond LLM-specific tooling. The group agreed they should remain open for now and be picked up by interested contributors when capacity allows, rather than falling entirely to Bruce; if they go stale they can be closed.

### PR [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366) — External-µ ML-DSA API (merged)

This PR has now been merged. Rodrigo noted that its implications for the oqs-provider are discussed further in the provider section below.

### CI redesign and test-suite refactoring

Bruce described two related workstreams he is pursuing:

**CI runtime reduction ([PR #2496](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2496), [issue #2497](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2497), [issue #2498](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2498)):** Michael has drafted a PR to bring down CI runtimes and opened an issue proposing a structured approach to what is tested and when — guided by the principle of running expensive or rarely-changing tests less frequently (e.g. weekly) and keeping per-commit checks fast. He asked that team members read the issue and comment on the proposed guidelines, which could also inform Bruce's own work.

**Test suite simplification (early brainstorming):** Bruce is exploring moving complex test logic — particularly around ACVP test vectors and similar boundary-case verification — out of C and into Python, using a simple C CLI program that exposes raw KEM/signature primitive inputs and outputs, with Python orchestrating the test logic. This is at a very early stage and may not be viable; Bruce asked not to be held to it. Michael noted that his CI work is largely orthogonal but the two may interact; he will leave an issue open for discussion as the brainstorming matures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Review [issue #2498](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2498) (CI redesign and review responsibility proposal) and comment on the proposed guidelines.

### Open PR backlog and prioritisation

Rodrigo noted that the liboqs open PR count currently stands at 24. He asked Bruce both how manageable the backlog feels and how best to distribute the review burden. Bruce reported that five PRs were merged last week but five new ones arrived, leaving the total unchanged. The main constraint is waiting time — either for Bruce to get to a PR, or for contributors to respond to review feedback. Among the larger items, Bruce identified the derandomised keypair generation for ML-DSA ([PR #2476](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2476)) as the most significant outstanding PR requiring focused attention; he has not yet been able to get to it. Rodrigo echoed that the ACVP tests PR ([#2386](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2386)) is also among the bigger items requiring design discussion.

On strategy, Bruce expressed no strong preference between tackling large PRs first or clearing small ones; given that it is early in the 0.17 cycle, he is comfortable letting smaller PRs wait while contributors respond. Rodrigo's guidance to the group: review whatever is most closely aligned with your area of familiarity, prioritise items related to Round 3 algorithm inclusion, and help Bruce by not leaving everything to him. He will also work toward labelling open PRs by priority and 0.17 relevance.

Michael questioned whether [PR #2476](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2476) (derandomised ML-DSA keypair generation) carries the `focus` label appropriately, since it concerns a standardised algorithm feature rather than advancing Round 3 onramp work. Bruce acknowledged he applied the label and will reconsider its use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Review open liboqs PRs in areas of familiarity; prioritise items relevant to Round 3 algorithm inclusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Label open liboqs PRs by priority and 0.17 milestone relevance to help guide reviewer effort.

### PR [#2417](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2417) — Build script (to be closed)

Basil flagged that the author of this PR had asked him privately whether it should be closed, following Bruce's review feedback suggesting rejection. Bruce confirmed his intention to close it — the script adds maintenance burden without providing meaningful benefit over existing build tooling — and noted that he thought this was already clear from his review comment. Rodrigo reminded Bruce that as a maintainer he is free to close PRs politely but decisively, stating the reason and leaving the door open for the contributor to re-engage with a revised approach. Basil will relay this to the author.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Close [PR #2417](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2417) with a polite explanation.

### Round 3 onramp algorithm outreach

Rodrigo reported that 4 of the 5 remaining submission teams have now responded positively to his outreach, with the latest reply coming from the ASCON-IT/AASHD team, who confirmed they are currently focused on preparing their August 14 resubmission but will be happy to collaborate on liboqs integration afterwards. The fifth team has not responded and Rodrigo is not optimistic, but will follow up once more after the deadline. The plan is to contact all willing teams after August 14 to organise a joint session (potentially in a status meeting slot or a separate call) to walk through the copy-from-upstream integration procedure and begin the inclusion work. Basil noted he plans to review the copy-from-upstream documentation over the summer to ensure it is accessible for submission teams doing their own integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** After August 14, contact the four willing submission teams to organise a joint onboarding session on the liboqs algorithm-integration procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Basil):** Review and update the copy-from-upstream integration documentation to ensure it is clear for external submission teams.

## OQS-OpenSSL 3 provider

### oqs-provider 0.12.0 release — remaining blockers

Rodrigo summarised the state of the provider. The milestone for the 0.12.0 release has been reactivated to track what needs to land before release. Two previously blocking items have been resolved: the CI is no longer testing against OpenSSL master (switched to OpenSSL 4.0.x per the decision from two weeks ago), and [PR #799](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/799) (add algorithm-overlap test) has been merged. The remaining known blocker is compatibility with OpenSSL master's changed caching and reference-counting behaviour for disabled algorithms ([issue #792](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/792)). Rodrigo has been in contact with the OpenSSL team, who acknowledged this is a bug on their side and are working on a fix. A separate deprecation introduced in OpenSSL 4.1 (not yet released) also needs handling before CI against master is restored ([issue #791](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/791)); this affects X.509-related certificate-handling calls. Rodrigo opened an issue this morning to track this.

Michael agreed that a clean master-CI pass is a desirable goal before the provider release but should not gate it indefinitely. The group's shared understanding remains: release once the OpenSSL-side fix is in and CI is green; the deprecation issue can be tracked as a separate follow-up if needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Monitor the OpenSSL-side fix for [issue #792](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/792) and re-enable master CI once it lands; proceed with the provider 0.12.0 release once CI is clean.

### PR [#802](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/802) — IANA code points for ML-KEM-512 hybrids

This PR adds IANA-assigned code points for `SecP256r1MLKEM512` and `MLKEM512X25519` ([issue #801](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/801)). It is the first provider hybrid in which the post-quantum algorithm name appears first in the identifier string, requiring a change to the logic that detects and parses the classical component. Rodrigo has reviewed the PR and found that the author's current solution avoids fixing the detection logic by not using the standardised name, which he does not consider acceptable. The fix will likely require updating how the provider identifies hybrid components — scanning the full name string rather than only the prefix. This work is ongoing; Rodrigo expects it to be resolved before the 0.12.0 release.

### PR [#778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778) — Avoid encoder hotpath in P-curve hybrid KEM keygen (closed)

This PR has been closed. Per last week's discussion, Norman independently reproduced the performance improvement and the underlying fix was found to be correct, despite the author's original benchmark numbers being presented incorrectly (decaps throughput was reported instead of keygen throughput). However, Rodrigo remained concerned about the AI-assisted provenance of parts of the PR and Michael noted residual uncertainty about one aspect of the implementation logic. Given these concerns and the higher-priority work currently in progress, the decision was made to close the PR. If the fix is still desired it can be resubmitted with clearer justification.

### PR [#786](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/786) — Harden GitHub Actions workflows (JP Lomas)

JP reported that this PR is ready to merge — it is housekeeping around action and container version pinning to prevent future CI failures caused by upstream container updates. Rodrigo confirmed it is on his list to review and merge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Review and merge [PR #786](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/786).

### Future direction: pure hybrid provider (Michael)

Michael raised a longer-term architectural idea: creating a standalone pure-hybrid provider (independent of the existing OQS codebase) that takes its post-quantum primitives from any source. He noted the current hybrid-handling code paths in oqs-provider are increasingly complex and were never intended to implement standardised hybrid schemes at production quality. He indicated a proof-of-concept is feasible. Rodrigo noted this would constitute a new sub-project and would be a topic for TSC discussion rather than something to decide in a status meeting. Both agreed it is worth putting on the TSC agenda.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Add the pure-hybrid-provider idea to the agenda for a future TSC meeting.

## Language wrappers

### liboqs-Go

JP reported submitting [PR #55](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/pull/55) to bring liboqs-Go to parity with liboqs 0.16.0. Douglas has already released liboqs-Go 0.15, resolving [issue #53](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/53). A new CI issue has also appeared ([issue #54](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/issues/54)): the External-µ ML-DSA changes now in liboqs main are causing failures in the Go wrapper's CI, since the wrapper has not yet been updated to handle the new API. PR #55 also addresses CI enhancements. JP confirmed that PR #55 is independent of [PR #2494](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2494) (the liboqs-side automated downstream release trigger); that is a proof-of-concept architectural change that would need broader discussion.

Michael mentioned [PR #2494](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2494) specifically, noting it looked relevant to the Go wrapper work. JP confirmed it is a proposal for automatically triggering wrapper releases when liboqs releases, and volunteered to review it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (maintainers):** Review [PR #55](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs-go/pull/55) (liboqs-Go 0.16.0 parity and CI enhancements).
